channel.title: Home on Daniel Nwazue
channel.description: Recent content in Home on Daniel Nwazue

item.title: 2.008 Yo-Yo
item.description:
Introduction

2.008 (Design and Manufacturing II) is MIT's production-scale manufacturing course. Rather than building one-off prototypes, each team designs a yo-yo and then manufactures 50 complete units using the same industrial processes found in real factories: injection molding, thermoforming, and CNC machining.

Goal: Design a yo-yo that meets all class constraints, can be manufactured repeatably at quantity, and holds together via a press fit with no glue or fasteners holding the halves together.

item.title: Cane Stabilizer
item.description:
Introduction

This project was part of MIT's Assistive Technology (AT) design program, where student teams are paired with a real co-designer to build something that addresses a specific need in their life. Our co-designer was Susan Bibbins, a retired children's psychologist living with fibromyalgia and hemiparesis, which leave her with one reliably functional hand. Susan uses a cane daily.

Problem statement: Current cane stabilizers are too bulky, heavy, and obstructive. We set out to create an external attachment to keep Susan's cane upright when not in use.

item.title: OX Truck Vibration Analysis
item.description:
As part of my engineering internship at OX Delivers in Leamington, England, I conducted a whole-body vibration (WBV) analysis comparing the OX4 truck's suspension performance against a Toyota Hilux, a well-established benchmark in the light commercial vehicle segment. The goal was to quantify driver and passenger vibration exposure across OX's test track routes and assess compliance with UK health and safety regulations.

Background

Whole-body vibration is a regulated occupational health risk in the UK under the Control of Vibration at Work Regulations 2005. Two thresholds govern how long a driver can be exposed before action is required:

item.title: 2.007 Robot
item.description:
Introduction

2.007 (Design and Manufacturing I) is MIT's core mechanical engineering design course. Each student spends the semester designing and building their own robot to compete on a shared game board. The 2025 theme was "Wicked FBD: Applying Gravity," based on the Wizard of Oz. Each round is two minutes long: a 30-second autonomous period followed by 90 seconds of remote control.

The game board had nine scoring opportunities: Fields of Poppies, Munchkinland Artifacts, Train to Oz, Hot Air Balloon, Oz Head Levers, Machinery's Grimmerie, Flying Monkeys, Oz Multiplier, and the Broom. Each had different point values, mechanical interfaces, and positions on the table.

item.title: Desktop Mill UROP
item.description:
The MIT Learning Engineering and Practice Group (LEAP), led by Dr. John Liu, is building the next generation of human-machine collaboration tools for manufacturing. Their flagship project, the Smart Mill, pairs a human operator with an intelligent robotic system mounted on a CNC vertical mill, with feedback delivered through extended reality (XR). I joined as a UROP researcher in Spring 2025, working directly under Dr. Rolando Bautista Montesano.

The Problem

Most human-machine collaboration systems today are built for simple, repetitive tasks. They rely on pre-programmed motions and can't adapt to real-time changes or interact dynamically with a human operator. This makes them poorly suited for high-mix, low-volume manufacturing, which is exactly the kind of flexible, skilled work that still requires human judgment. The Smart Mill addresses this by combining sensing, cognitive modeling, and XR feedback into a system that learns from expert operators and helps train new ones.

item.title: Small Projects
item.description:
E-Scooter Blind-Spot Detector

During MIT's IAP (Independent Activities Period) in January 2024, I received a $400 MIT grant to build anything I wanted. A friend had recently purchased an e-scooter, and after thinking about how exposed you feel on one, I decided to build a real-time blind-spot detection system.

The system uses ultrasonic sensors (HC-SR04) mounted to a backpack, controlled by an Arduino Nano. I chose ultrasonic over infrared or LiDAR for the cost and reliability in noisy environments. It triggers two alert levels: a medium alarm when something comes within 10 feet, and a high-pitched alarm plus haptic vibration (via a Wii controller motor) when within 5 feet. I chose to mount the sensors to the rider instead of the scooter, to allow it to work across different vehicles.

item.title: Refrigerated Seed Dispenser
item.description:
In MIT's 2.00 Introductory Product Design course, our team was assigned the theme of "Gathering" and matched with volunteers from the Somerville Gardening Club and Somerville Growing Center, two organizations focused on promoting native plant cultivation in the community.

The Problem

The volunteers were already running community events and handing out seed packets, but they repeatedly encountered the same issue: many native Boston seeds require cold stratification before germination can occur. These seeds naturally experience a cold winter period in the ground, but without mimicking that process artificially, they simply won't sprout when planted in spring.

item.title: Boat Thruster Shield
item.description: MIT Arcturus is a student team that competes in RoboBoat, an international student competition where teams design, build, and operate autonomous surface vessels (ASVs) that navigate and complete tasks on the water. For the team's new flagship boat, I designed a thruster shield structure that protects the thrusters from impact, secures them to the hull, and supports the full weight of the boat when it's resting on the ground between runs.

item.title: About
item.description:
Hey there! I'm Daniel, a mechanical engineering student at MIT studying Controls, Instrumentation, and Robotics (Course 2A‑6). I'm passionate about designing and building systems that integrate mechanical design, manufacturing, sensing, and intelligent control. I enjoy working through the full engineering process—from concept and CAD to fabrication and testing.

Right now, I'm designing and manufacturing 50 yo-yos for MIT's 2.008 Design and Manufacturing II class, where students develop a product and produce it in quantity using industrial manufacturing methods. My experience includes:
